title: Why Strait of Hormuz no longer rattles oil markets as much: IMF
gist: IMF Middle East director Jihad Azour tells Al-Monitor that new bypass infrastructure (Saudi's East-West pipeline, UAE port expansion) and a roughly 5-million-bpd demand adjustment have made oil markets far less sensitive to Strait of Hormuz disruptions than earlier in the US-Iran conflict, though Iraq, Iran and Qatar remain acutely exposed and the Gulf's AI investment boom is emerging as a distinct medium-term diversification story.

themes.theme: Hormuz bypass infrastructure blunts the shock

insights.claim: Saudi Arabia largely paused oil shipments from its Gulf export terminals at Ras Tanura and Juaymah in March 2026 after tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz plunged following Iranian attacks, redirecting crude exports through its East-West pipeline to the Red Sea port of Yanbu instead.
insights.speaker: Jack Dutton
insights.so_what: Saudi Arabia has a working physical bypass of Hormuz already in operation, materially capping the price impact of further strait disruptions on Saudi barrels.
insights.locator: Saudi Arabia, the region's largest oil producer

insights.claim: DP World plans to build a new port and container terminal on the UAE's east coast to reduce Dubai's reliance on Jebel Ali and bypass the Strait of Hormuz, per Financial Times reporting.
insights.speaker: Jack Dutton
insights.so_what: Watch DP World's east-coast port project as a multi-year structural reduction in UAE logistics dependence on Hormuz.
insights.locator: The United Arab Emirates is also expanding alternatives

insights.claim: IMF's Jihad Azour says new channels built to bypass the Strait of Hormuz have increased available oil supply and made the oil market's reaction to renewed tensions much less volatile than earlier in the conflict, though price and inflation risk remain the main residual challenge.
insights.speaker: Jihad Azour
insights.so_what: The core investable claim of the piece: fade oversized oil-price spikes on Hormuz headlines relative to earlier-2026 reactions, since physical bypass capacity has structurally grown.
insights.quote: What has reduced the risks so far are the new channels developed to bypass the Strait of Hormuz, which allowed more supply to reach markets and reduced pressure on prices.
insights.locator: Azour said the expansion of alternative export infrastructure

insights.claim: Global oil demand has adjusted downward by almost 5 million barrels per day as a result of the conflict, and new suppliers have entered the market, further reducing sensitivity to Hormuz disruptions.
insights.speaker: Jihad Azour
insights.so_what: A ~5 million bpd demand-side adjustment plus new supply is a structural rebalancing that dampens future Hormuz-driven price spikes independent of pipeline capacity.
insights.locator: We have to take into account that global oil demand has adjusted

insights.claim: Countries with geographic access to seas outside the Strait of Hormuz, such as Oman, or with built bypass infrastructure, such as Saudi Arabia's pipeline and the UAE's export pipeline, have been better protected from the shock than others.
insights.speaker: Jihad Azour
insights.so_what: A simple screen for Gulf-conflict resilience: geography plus bypass infrastructure predicts which oil exporters outperform during Hormuz stress.
insights.locator: A country like Oman has access to the seas

themes.theme: Uneven country exposure and IMF growth downgrades

insights.claim: The IMF cut its global economic growth forecast slightly from 3.1% to 3%, citing the Middle East conflict.
insights.speaker: Jack Dutton
insights.so_what: A modest 10bp global growth cut confirms the conflict is a drag but not (yet) a global recessionary shock per IMF's own numbers.
insights.locator: Last week, the IMF cut its global economic growth forecast

insights.claim: Growth downgrades for Oman and Saudi Arabia have been relatively limited compared to much larger downgrades for Qatar, Iraq and Iran, according to Azour.
insights.speaker: Jihad Azour
insights.so_what: A clear cross-country ranking of conflict exposure: long Oman/Saudi resilience narratives, short Qatar/Iraq/Iran ones on further escalation.
insights.locator: for countries like Oman or Saudi Arabia, the downgrade was relatively limited

insights.claim: More than 90% of Iraq's exports are channeled through the Strait of Hormuz, and the country's financial buffers are relatively limited given increased public spending in recent years, making it highly exposed to prolonged disruption.
insights.speaker: Jihad Azour
insights.so_what: Iraq is the single most Hormuz-dependent major exporter in the IMF's own account (>90%) with weak buffers — a top downside-risk name to monitor.
insights.locator: more than 90% of Iraq's exports are channeled

insights.claim: Qatar is exposed to Hormuz disruption because its LNG exports are channeled through the strait, but it has diversified its oil and gas investments and holds a high level of financial buffers, partially offsetting the risk.
insights.speaker: Jihad Azour
insights.so_what: Qatar combines high headline Hormuz exposure with strong balance-sheet buffers — a more nuanced risk case than Iraq's, worth distinguishing in country risk models.
insights.locator: Other countries, like Qatar, are exposed

themes.theme: Transmission channels to fragile and low-income states

insights.claim: Azour identifies three main transmission channels of the conflict to the wider region: flows of oil and gas, remittances, and food, with impact concentrated in low-income countries and fragile states.
insights.speaker: Jihad Azour
insights.so_what: A three-channel framework (energy, remittances, food) for tracing conflict spillover gives investors a checklist for screening frontier-market vulnerability beyond direct oil exposure.
insights.locator: There are three main channels of transmission to countries

insights.claim: IMF summer projections show that after the Gulf countries, Iraq and Iran form the group most negatively affected by the conflict.
insights.speaker: Jihad Azour
insights.so_what: Confirms Iraq and Iran as the IMF's own top-ranked at-risk economies outside the immediate Gulf producers.
insights.locator: our projections for the summer show

insights.claim: Pakistan, Sri Lanka and Indonesia have high dependence on hydrocarbon imports from the Gulf region and are among the most exposed non-Gulf countries to a prolonged Hormuz disruption.
insights.speaker: Jihad Azour
insights.so_what: Names three specific Asian importer economies as high-vulnerability watch-list names for energy-import-driven balance-of-payments stress.
insights.locator: Countries like Pakistan, Sri Lanka and Indonesia

insights.claim: China, Korea and Japan have developed alternative energy sources and built up reserves, making them large hydrocarbon importers that are nonetheless better insulated from Hormuz disruption than countries lacking such buffers.
insights.speaker: Jihad Azour
insights.so_what: Distinguishes resilient large Asian importers (China, Korea, Japan) from vulnerable ones, refining the naive assumption that all major oil importers face equal Hormuz risk.
insights.locator: Other countries, although they are large importers

insights.claim: African and other food-insecure countries face a double exposure from the conflict: rising fertilizer and food prices, and reduced capacity to access supply.
insights.speaker: Jihad Azour
insights.so_what: Fertilizer/food transmission is a distinct, under-the-radar risk channel (vs. the headline oil-price story) for African sovereign and agricultural exposure.
insights.locator: African countries, or countries that have already been facing fragilities

insights.claim: Iran's economy was already suffering hyperinflation, a massive currency devaluation and negative growth from years of maximum-pressure sanctions before the war began, and the IMF expects the conflict to further destroy infrastructure and worsen growth and inflation.
insights.speaker: Jihad Azour
insights.so_what: Iran enters this new war phase from a position of pre-existing macro fragility (hyperinflation, negative growth), implying limited fiscal or monetary space to absorb further shocks.
insights.locator: Iran has been negatively affected even before the war

insights.claim: Azour says stabilizing Iran's economy will require the right policy mix to rein in rising prices, implying current Iranian policy is inadequate to the task.
insights.speaker: Jihad Azour
insights.so_what: IMF's framing suggests Iran will need significant post-conflict policy reform (and likely external support) to stabilize, a signal for any future Iran-related capital markets access.
insights.locator: It requires the right policies to rein in increasing prices

themes.theme: Gulf AI investment as a diversification bet

insights.claim: Saudi Arabia, the UAE and Qatar are, according to Azour, among the top-tier countries globally in AI readiness and investment, with levels comparable to advanced economies, and AI has become "mainstream" in those economies.
insights.speaker: Jihad Azour
insights.so_what: IMF is effectively ranking Saudi Arabia, UAE and Qatar alongside advanced economies on AI investment/readiness — a bullish structural signal for Gulf AI-linked capex and data-center demand.
insights.locator: there are at least three countries that are in the top league

insights.claim: Azour expects increased AI investment to accelerate economic diversification and productivity in Gulf economies, but also to create labor-market inclusion challenges, especially for youth.
insights.speaker: Jihad Azour
insights.so_what: Frames Gulf AI investment as a double-edged diversification tool: productivity upside paired with a youth-employment risk that governments will need to manage politically.
insights.locator: the more we see investment in this, the more we see potential increase in productivity

insights.claim: Gulf countries' data-center buildouts have relied on access to cheap energy, but emerging alternative-energy technologies and geopolitical risk may delay some of those planned investments.
insights.speaker: Jihad Azour
insights.so_what: Geopolitical risk (i.e., the ongoing war) is now cited by the IMF as a direct headwind to Gulf AI data-center investment timelines, not just an oil-market issue.
insights.locator: some of the countries were banking on access to cheap energy

insights.claim: GCC countries have made heavy overseas AI-related investments in the US and Asia, which Azour expects to trickle down and benefit their home economies through diversification.
insights.speaker: Jihad Azour
insights.so_what: Gulf sovereign AI capital is flowing abroad first (US/Asia) with domestic diversification benefits expected only indirectly and later — relevant for timing any domestic Gulf AI-linked equity or infrastructure thesis.
insights.locator: some of them have been heavily invested internationally

insights.claim: Azour says it will take "a few years" to see how the AI-driven industrial revolution changes Gulf economies and to determine winners and losers globally, and low- and middle-income Middle East countries need to accelerate tech-infrastructure adoption or risk falling behind.
insights.speaker: Jihad Azour
insights.so_what: Sets investor expectations: tangible domestic payoff from Gulf AI bets is a multi-year story, not a near-term catalyst, while poorer regional economies risk a widening tech gap.
insights.locator: I think we need to wait a few years

insights.claim: A fragile ceasefire had been in place since April 2026 and a US-Iran memorandum of understanding was signed on June 17 to end the nearly five-month war, but violence flared again the week before this interview after tankers were attacked in the Strait of Hormuz.
insights.speaker: Jack Dutton
insights.so_what: Establishes that even the IMF's more sanguine market-impact read was made against a backdrop of a ceasefire that had already once broken down — the underlying conflict risk remains live.
insights.locator: Despite a fragile ceasefire agreement that has been in place

insights.claim: The Strait of Hormuz, lying between Iran, Oman and the UAE, handled about one-fifth of global oil and LNG shipments before the conflict began.
insights.speaker: Jack Dutton
insights.so_what: The one-fifth global oil/LNG baseline is the reference point against which all the bypass-infrastructure mitigation described in this piece should be measured.
insights.locator: The strategic waterway, which lies between Iran, Oman and the UAE
